title: Pillars of Creation
description: Towering columns of cool interstellar gas and dust in the Eagle Nebula (M16), roughly 6,500 light-years away. These pillars are nurseries for newborn stars, sculpted by intense ultraviolet light from nearby hot stars.

title: Carina Nebula
description: A dynamic, star-forming region about 7,500 light-years away. The nebula contains at least a dozen brilliant stars that are 50 to 100 times the mass of our Sun, including the famous Eta Carinae binary system.

title: Hubble Ultra Deep Field
description: One of the most profound images ever taken. This tiny patch of sky in the constellation Fornax reveals approximately 10,000 galaxies, stretching back to within a few hundred million years of the Big Bang.
credit: NASA, ESA, S. Beckwith (STScI), HUDF Team

title: Orion Nebula
description: The nearest stellar nursery to Earth at roughly 1,344 light-years away. This mosaic reveals over 3,000 stars in various stages of formation within a turbulent cloud of gas and dust spanning 12 light-years.

title: Whirlpool Galaxy
description: The grand spiral galaxy M51, located 31 million light-years away, locked in a gravitational embrace with its smaller companion NGC 5195. The sharp spiral arms are sites of star formation compressed by the interaction.
credit: NASA, ESA, S. Beckwith (STScI), Hubble Heritage Team

title: Eagle Nebula (Stellar Spire)
description: A towering column of gas and dust in the Eagle Nebula, standing 9.5 light-years tall — about twice the distance from our Sun to the nearest star. Stars are being born at its tip within dense pockets called EGGs.
